Nobody hands you a senior title. Your code earns it, one small decision at a time, long before anyone notices.
I used to think seniority was about knowing more. It is not. It is about a long list of small, boring, consistent choices, repeated so often they stop looking like choices at all.
Twenty-one of them. No fluff, no filler, just the exact habits that quietly separate code that works from code that lasts. Read this once, then keep it open next to your editor for a week.
Architecture and Structure
1. Default to standalone components
New Angular code should reach for standalone components first, not NgModules. Fewer files, fewer decorators to keep in sync, and a dependency list you can see directly on the component itself.
@Component({
selector: 'user-card',
standalone: true,
imports: [CommonModule],
template: `...`
})
export class UserCardComponent {}2. Split smart and dumb components
A component either owns data and logic, or it renders what it is given. Mixing both inside one class is how a simple screen turns into four hundred lines nobody wants to touch.
